issues.original: In 1912, Ronald Fisher introduced **maximum likelihood estimation** in a short paper that quietly redefined statistics. His insight was almost embarrassingly simple: *if a parameter setting makes the observed data extremely likely, that parameter setting is probably right*. Almost every modern learning algorithm — from logistic regression to large language models — is a descendant of this idea.
issues.fixed: In 1912, Ronald Fisher introduced **maximum likelihood estimation** in a short paper that quietly redefined statistics. His insight was almost embarrassingly simple: *if a parameter setting makes the observed data extremely likely, it is probably correct*. Almost every modern learning algorithm — from logistic regression to large language models — descends from this idea.

issues.original: But likelihood alone is not enough. To use it we need a vocabulary for uncertainty (probability spaces, distributions), guarantees that empirical quantities track population ones (laws of large numbers, central limit theorem), and tools for incorporating prior knowledge (Bayesian inference). This article assembles those pieces into a coherent foundation for everything that follows.
issues.fixed: But likelihood alone is not enough. We need a vocabulary for uncertainty (probability spaces, distributions), guarantees that empirical quantities track population ones (laws of large numbers, central limit theorem), and tools for incorporating prior knowledge (Bayesian inference). This article assembles these pieces into a coherent foundation for what follows.

issues.original: Probability theory rests on a triplet [FORMULA]. - **Sample space** [FORMULA] — the set of all possible outcomes of an experiment. - **[FORMULA]-algebra** [FORMULA] — the collection of 'events' we are allowed to assign probabilities to. It is closed under complement and countable union. - **Probability measure** [FORMULA] — satisfies non-negativity, normalization [FORMULA], and countable additivity for disjoint events.

issues.original: Named entity recognition, POS tagging, information extraction — every one of these tasks asks you to label each element of a sequence. HMMs (Part 15) attack this problem **generatively** by modelling the joint distribution [FORMULA], but to make the joint factorise they pay a steep price: each observation is assumed independent of everything except its own hidden label.
issues.fixed: Named entity recognition, POS tagging, and information extraction all require labeling each element of a sequence. HMMs (Part 15) tackle this problem **generatively** by modeling the joint distribution [FORMULA]. However, to factorize the joint, they assume each observation is independent of everything except its own hidden label.

issues.original: In real text, whether *bank* is a noun or a verb depends on the preceding word, the following word, the suffix, capitalisation, dictionary lookups — all of these features at once.
issues.fixed: In real text, whether *bank* is a noun or a verb depends on the preceding and following words, the suffix, capitalization, and dictionary lookups — all these features together.

issues.original: Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) drop the generative ambition entirely and model [FORMULA] directly. Once you no longer need a generative story for [FORMULA], you can pile on as many overlapping features of [FORMULA] as you like.
issues.fixed: Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) abandon the generative approach and model [FORMULA] directly. Without needing a generative story for [FORMULA], you can add as many overlapping features of [FORMULA] as you like.

issues.fixed: HMM models the **joint** probability of observations [FORMULA] and labels [FORMULA]. To predict labels, you use Bayes' rule: [FORMULA]. Two assumptions make the joint tractable but also restrictive:

issues.original: The Markov assumption is mostly fine. The observation independence assumption is the killer: it forbids any feature that looks at neighbouring tokens, suffixes, or gazetteers conditionally on the current label.
issues.fixed: The Markov assumption is mostly fine. The observation independence assumption, however, is the real issue: it forbids any feature that considers neighboring tokens, suffixes, or gazetteers based on the current label.

issues.original: The Maximum Entropy Markov Model (MEMM) was the natural intermediate step between HMM and CRF: MEMM is discriminative *and* allows arbitrary features of [FORMULA], but it normalises **locally** at each position. This causes the **label-bias problem**: states with few outgoing transitions concentrate probability mass simply because their per-position softmax has fewer competitors — regardless of whether the observation supports them.
issues.fixed: The Maximum Entropy Markov Model (MEMM) was the natural intermediate step between HMM and CRF. MEMM is discriminative and allows arbitrary features of [FORMULA], but it normalizes **locally** at each position. This leads to the **label-bias problem**: states with few outgoing transitions concentrate probability mass because their per-position softmax has fewer competitors, regardless of whether the observation supports them.

issues.original: Even outside sequence problems the same trade-off applies. A generative model spends parameters on [FORMULA] — the smooth purple density on the left — which is wasted capacity if all you care about is the boundary. A discriminative model spends every parameter directly on the conditional decision boundary — the sharp diagonal contour on the right. Naive Bayes vs Logistic Regression and HMM vs CRF are exactly the same trade-off lifted to two scales.
issues.fixed: Even outside sequence problems, the same trade-off applies. A generative model allocates parameters to [FORMULA] — the smooth purple density on the left — which is wasted capacity if you only care about the boundary. A discriminative model uses every parameter directly on the conditional decision boundary — the sharp diagonal contour on the right. Naive Bayes vs. Logistic Regression and HMM vs. CRF are the same trade-off, just at different scales.
